claims.claim_text: Production-ready ML workflows should use reproducible pipelines with repeatable, reusable steps for data preparation, training, and scoring.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. A Practical Project Shape for the Transition | paragraphs on separable code, config, outputs, and automation
claims.rationale: The Azure MLOps model-management doc explicitly says reproducible ML pipelines define repeatable and reusable steps for data preparation, training, and scoring.

claims.claim_text: Model evaluation and validation should compare a candidate against a named baseline or threshold before promotion.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Separating Training, Evaluation, and Serving | paragraphs beginning 'A clean evaluation path should compare the candidate to a baseline'
claims.rationale: Google's MLOps architecture guide states that model evaluation produces quality metrics and model validation confirms performance is better than a baseline before deployment.

claims.claim_text: Artifact governance requires versioned models plus metadata and lineage so the team can track what was trained, why it changed, and where it is deployed.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Artifact Governance and Promotion Readiness | paragraphs on lineage, ownership, and governance
claims.rationale: The Azure doc explicitly covers model registration, versioning, metadata tags, and end-to-end lineage for ML lifecycle governance.

claims.claim_text: A production serving path needs an explicit scoring interface, runtime environment, deployment settings, and monitoring rather than only ad hoc interactive code.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Separating Training, Evaluation, and Serving | serving-contract paragraphs and serving concern table
claims.rationale: The Azure online-endpoints doc specifies model files, scoring script, environment, instance/scaling settings, and monitoring as deployment requirements.

claims.claim_text: Shadow testing and limited rollout are distinct production-validation stages: mirrored traffic can test a new deployment without affecting user-visible results, while partial traffic routing supports limited rollout.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Artifact Governance and Promotion Readiness | paragraphs defining shadow testing and limited rollout
claims.rationale: The Azure online-endpoints doc explicitly describes traffic mirroring or shadowing and percentage-based traffic routing for blue/green rollout.

claims.claim_text: Model candidate documentation should include intended use, limitations, training parameters, datasets, and evaluation results.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Artifact Governance and Promotion Readiness | candidate-evidence paragraphs; Sources list entry for Model Cards
claims.rationale: Hugging Face's Model Cards documentation explicitly lists intended uses, limitations, training information, datasets, and evaluation results as core contents.

further_reading.title: MLOps: Continuous Delivery and Automation Pipelines in Machine Learning
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds the module's separation of experimentation, validation, deployment, and operational monitoring in a recognized MLOps framework.

further_reading.title: MLOps Model Management with Azure Machine Learning
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for lineage, model registration, metadata, governance, and reproducible pipeline concepts discussed in the module.

further_reading.title: Model Cards
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly supports the module's emphasis on making model candidates reviewable through documented datasets, evaluation evidence, and limitations.

notes: Most of the module is sound pedagogical framing rather than citation-heavy factual prose; code blocks, diagrams, tables, quiz items, and hands-on steps were intentionally excluded per the brief.
